This caused the client to exhaust its retry budget and fail over to fallback behavior, resulting in a cascading failure of downstream services.

The misconfiguration was introduced in commit 1234567890 on branch feature/parallel-processing. The commit message did not clearly indicate the retry budget change, and the associated test cases did not cover the elevated latency scenario.

02:27:00 UTC — Initial failure reports start coming in from customer-facing dashboards. I (on-call engineer) acknowledge the issue and start investigating.

02:29:00 UTC — I identify the payment-service client as the likely root cause and begin reviewing recent changes.

02:32:00 UTC — I find the commit with the retry budget change (1234567890 on feature/parallel-processing) and confirm it was deployed to production 3 hours earlier.

02:34:00 UTC — I attempt to revert commit 1234567890, but I accidentally revert commit 0987654321 (a unrelated fix for logging) at 14:23 UTC. This change does not address the retry budget issue and makes no operational difference.

02:36:00 UTC — The outage continues, and I realize the revert was incorrect. I immediately revert commit 0987654321 and attempt to revert commit 1234567890 correctly this time.

02:40:00 UTC — The correct revert takes effect, and the payment-service client begins retrying successfully. The cascading failure subsides, and downstream services recover.

02:42:00 UTC — I confirm all services are stable and begin documenting the incident.

02:54:00 UTC — The outage is fully resolved, and I declare it over.

3. **Update the canary deploy pipeline** to detect changes in retry budgets and trigger additional latency testing before promoting to production. (Owner: platform engineering team)

These gaps in our testing and review processes contributed to the delay in identifying and resolving the issue. We will prioritize addressing these gaps to reduce the likelihood of similar outages in the future.
